title: Hear Seven Hours of Women Making Electronic Music (1938ã2014)
description:
Image via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
A numôÙber of years ago, in a post on the pioôÙneerôÙing comôÙposôÙer of the origôÙiôÙnal DocôÙtor Who theme, we wrote tha
body:
Image via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
A numôÙber of years ago, in a post on the pioôÙneerôÙing comôÙposôÙer of the origôÙiôÙnal DocôÙtor Who theme, we wrote that ãthe earôÙly era of experôÙiôÙmenôÙtal elecôÙtronôÙic music belonged to Delia DerôÙbyshire.ã Derbyshireãwho almost gave Paul McCartôÙney a verôÙsion of ãYesôÙterôÙdayã with an elecôÙtronôÙic backôÙing in place of stringsãhelped invent the earôÙly elecôÙtronôÙic music of the sixôÙties through her work with the RadioôÙphonôÙic WorkôÙshop, the sound effects labôÙoôÙraôÙtoôÙry of the BBC. She went on to form one of the most influôÙenôÙtial, if largeôÙly obscure, elecôÙtronôÙic acts of the decade, White Noise. And yet, callôÙing the earôÙly eras of elecôÙtronôÙic music hers is an exagôÙgerôÙaôÙtion. Of course her many colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtors deserve menôÙtion, as well as musiôÙcians like Bruce Haack, Pierre HenôÙry, Kraftwerk, BriôÙan Eno, and so many othôÙers. But what gets almost comôÙpleteôÙly left out of many hisôÙtoôÙries of elecôÙtronôÙic music, as with so many othôÙer hisôÙtoôÙries, is the promiôÙnent role so many women besides DerôÙbyshire played in the develôÙopôÙment of the sounds we now hear around us all the time.
In recogôÙniôÙtion of this fact, musiôÙcian, DJ, and ãescaped housewife/schoolteacherã BarôÙbara GoldôÙen devotôÙed two episodesô of her KPFA radio proôÙgram ãCrack oã Dawnã to women in elecôÙtronôÙic music, once in 2010 and again in 2013. She shares each broadôÙcast with co-host Jon LeiôÙdeckôÙer (ãWobôÙblyã), and in each segôÙment, the two banôÙter in casuôÙal radio show style, offerôÙing hisôÙtoôÙry and conôÙtext for each musiôÙcian and comôÙposôÙer. HighôÙlightôÙed on Ubuãs forôÙmer TwitôÙter stream, the first show, ãWomen in ElecôÙtronôÙic Music 1938ã1982 Part 1ã (above) gives DerôÙbyshire her due, with three tracks from her, includôÙing the DocôÙtor Who theme.
It also includes music from twenôÙty oneô othôÙer comôÙposers, beginôÙning with Clara RockôÙmore, a refinôÙer and popôÙuôÙlarôÙizôÙer of the theremin, that weird instruôÙment designed to simôÙuôÙlate a high, tremuôÙlous human voice. Also feaôÙtured is Wendy Carlosãs ãTimesteps,ã an origôÙiôÙnal piece from her A ClockôÙwork Orange score.
The secôÙond show, above, fills in sevôÙerôÙal gaps in the origôÙiôÙnal broadôÙcast and ãcould easôÙiôÙly be six hoursã says co-host LeiôÙdeckôÙer, givôÙen the sheer amount of elecôÙtronôÙic music out there comôÙposed and recordôÙed by women over the past sevôÙenôÙtyô years. This show includes one of our host Goldenãs own comôÙpoôÙsiôÙtions, ãMelody SumôÙnôÙer CarôÙnaôÙhan,ã as well as music from LauôÙrie AnderôÙson and musique conôÙcrete comôÙposôÙer Doris Hays. These two broadôÙcasts alone covôÙer an enorôÙmous range of stylôÙisôÙtic and techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal ground, but for even more discoôÙgraphôÙiôÙcal hisôÙtoôÙry of women in elecôÙtronôÙic music, see the playlist below, comôÙpiled by ãNerdôÙgirlã Antye Greie-RipatôÙti. ComôÙmisôÙsioned by Club TransôÙmeôÙdiôÙale Berlin, the mix includes such well-known names as Yoko Ono, Bjork, and M.I.A., as well as foreôÙmothôÙers DerôÙbyshire and CarôÙlos, and dozens more.
In lieu of the radio-show chatôÙter of GoldôÙen and LeiôÙdeckôÙer, we have Greie-Ripattiãs post detailôÙing each artistãs time periôÙod, counôÙtry of oriôÙgin, and conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to elecôÙtronôÙic music hisôÙtoôÙry. Many of the comôÙposers repôÙreôÙsentôÙed here worked for major radio and film stuôÙdios, scored feaôÙture films (like 1956ãs ForôÙbidôÙden PlanôÙet), inventôÙed and innoôÙvatôÙed new instruôÙments and techôÙniques, wrote for orchesôÙtras, and passed on their knowlôÙedge as eduôÙcaôÙtors and proôÙducôÙers. Greie-Ripattiãs page quotes a DanôÙish elecôÙtronôÙic proôÙducôÙer and perôÙformer sayôÙing ãthere is a lot of women in elecôÙtronôÙic musicãÎ invisôÙiôÙble women.ã Thanks to efforts like hers and Goldenãs, these pioôÙneerôÙing creôÙators need no longer go unseen or, more imporôÙtantôÙly, unheard.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Meet Delia DerôÙbyshire, theô Dr. Whoô ComôÙposôÙer Who Almost Turned The BeaôÙtôÙlesã ãYesôÙterôÙdayã Into EarôÙly ElecôÙtronôÙiôÙca
Watch ComôÙposôÙer Wendy CarôÙlos Demo an OrigôÙiôÙnal Moog SynôÙtheôÙsizôÙer (1989)
Meet Four Women Who PioôÙneered ElecôÙtronôÙic Music: Daphne Oram, LauôÙrie Spiegel, ûliane Radigue & Pauline OlivôÙeros
New DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtaryô SisôÙters with TranôÙsisôÙtorsô Tells the StoôÙry of ElecôÙtronôÙic Musicãs Female PioôÙneers
Mr. Rogers IntroôÙduces Kids to ExperôÙiôÙmenôÙtal ElecôÙtronôÙic Music by Bruce Haack & Esther NelôÙson (1968)
Hearô ElecôÙtronôÙic LadyôÙland, a MixôÙtape FeaôÙturôÙing 55 Tracks from 35 PioôÙneerôÙing Women in ElecôÙtronôÙic Music

Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Monty Python Philosophy Soccer Match: The Ancient Greeks Versus the Germans
description:

Today, weãre revisôÙitôÙing a clasôÙsic MonôÙty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich
body:
in Comedy, Sports |   	March 13th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Today, weãre revisôÙitôÙing a clasôÙsic MonôÙty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich Olympics. The event is a football/soccer match, pitôÙting GerôÙman philosoôÙphers against Greek philosoôÙphers. On the one side, the GerôÙmans ã Hegel, NietôÙzsche, Kant, Marx and, um, Franz BeckôÙenôÙbauer. On the othôÙer side, Archimedes, Socrates, PlaôÙto and the rest of the gang. The refôÙerôÙee? ConôÙfuôÙcius. Of course.
Enjoy!
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: A Visit to TomûÀs Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice
description: What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? TomûÀs Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21,
body:
What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? TomûÀs Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21, in which filmmakers visit his Berlin studio and examine the machinations of his collaborative practice, extending from a team of people to the tiny critters beneath our feet.
Saraceno continually considers how humans occupy space and how such environments inform the ways we connect with the world around us. This short documentary, which is part of the ãRealms of the Realã episode, reviews several of the artistãs projects, from his suspended installations to his more participatory community projects.



Several artworks presented in the film have been previously featured on Colossal, and the film offers insight into the evolution of Saracenoãs thinking over several years. Much of his work strives for connection and empathy building, which he explains through the structure of the web. ãItãs really trying to extend the ability of understanding who is our family, right? Who is our brothers, sisters, and grandfathers?ã he says. ãBy allowing others to admire these incredible webs, they will become more empathetic.ã
Find more Art21 films on YouTube, along with some of our favorites previously on Colossal.

schema.headline: A Visit to TomûÀs Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice

title: In 'Life Forms,' Janny Baek Imagines a Speculative Landscape
description:
Joy Machine is pleased to presentô Life Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change?
body:
Joy Machine is pleased to presentô Life Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change? With fear, excitement, or uncertainty? As Janny Baek builds sculptural ceramics of speculative beings and imagined landscapes, she grapples with these questions. The work follows its own dream logic, one that accepts incongruity and dissonance as necessary to play and experimentation. Marbling hunks of colored clay, coiling bases, and molding a singular material into something new is part of an exploratory practice that embraces transformation and its often strange outcomes.
Detail of ãDream Stateã (2024)
Life Formsô emerges from this dual meaning, invoking both the act of creation and the fantastical works it produces. Neither wholly abstract nor representational, Baekãs sculptures draw on natural structures and processes and invite us to question how we interpret the world around us. Recognizable forms like open blossoms, birds, and creatures are met with the unexpected. These lively components make even the more abstract works appear animate, like ambiguous organisms that might decide to scuttle away. They evoke something primordial and yet are exhilaratingly new.
Baek paints in the way she sketches, as a means of developing ideas and visualizing their potential. For her ceramics, she incorporates hand building alongside the Japanese pottery technique known asô nerikomi, which involves splicing and designing patterns with strips of colored clay. ãMy material choices are a way of thinking about natural processes: color gradients as the continuous nature of change, a multitude of colors as potential, abundance, and vitality, and patterns as signals and communications,ã she says.
Hovering between worlds, Baekãs work populates a speculative environment in which beings morph, mutate, and blossom, their individual features forming an otherworldly lineage thatãs recognizable but not identical. While ãtemporarily and imperfectly captured in a moment of many possible transformations,ã the works beckon us into a world in which change is not only inevitable but also the most alluring proposition.
Life Formsô is Baekãs Chicago debut. An opening reception will be held on March 20, and the artist will be present.ô See more from Baek previously on Colossal.
ãFuture Blossomsã (2023), colored porcelain, 13 x 13 x 12 inches
ãLandscapeã (2025), colored stoneware with glazed interior, 15 x 8 x 8 inches
Detail of ãDream Stateã (2024)
ãDream State 2ã (2025), stoneware, colored slip, porcelain, and glaze, 21 x 17 x 16 inches
Detail of ãLandscapeã (2025)
ãPlant Lifeã (2025), stoneware, colored stoneware, and glaze, 21.5 x 15 x 15 inches
Detail of ãPlant Lifeã (2025)

title: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints
description: Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled
body:
Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled Off the Grid emerged from a familiar yet annoying scenario: owning an older device that can no longer be updated, rendering it practically unusable. Endrullatãs frustration led him to a moment of impulsivity as he pushed his device through a printing press, coincidentally discovering the distinctive technique.
ãOne of the most interesting aspects of the process is how clearly the progressive destruction of the devices becomes visible after each print,ã the artist explains. With each pass through the device becomes increasingly altered, revealing details about its internal structures and their previous ownersãworn keys that must have been used the most, traces of sticker residue, or remnants of webcam covers. Wiping off ink in between prints also becomes more challenging throughout the process, as glass screens and touchpads begin to crack.

The artistãs studio printing press is just about one hundred years old, introducing a fascinating contrast between the longevity of toolsãboth robust and enduring or short-lived, perhaps even reminiscent of planned obsolescence. ãThe series reflects on materiality, consumption, and the hidden architectures ofdigital devices,ã he says. ãAlthough I might also simply enjoy the sound a laptop makes the first time it runs through the press.ã
Endrullat hopes to experiment with printing entire computer setups, complete with mice, keyboards, monitors, and more, which would eventually call for a different type of press. Take a peek at the artistãs process and find more work on his Instagram.

schema.headline: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints

title: You'll Need a Magnifying Glass to Read Some of the World's Smallest Books at the V&A
description: At Windsor Castle, a one-of-a-kind architectural marvel isnãt a structural part of the building itself or even a full-size feature. Here, youãll f
body:
At Windsor Castle, a one-of-a-kind architectural marvel isnãt a structural part of the building itself or even a full-size feature. Here, youãll find Queen Maryãs Dollsã House, widely regarded as the largest and most famous in the world. Designed by architect Sir Edwin Lutyens, the house was built between 1921 and 1924 and contains items and furnishings conceived of by hundreds of the leading craftspeople and artisans of the day.
Queen Mary, consort to King George V between 1910 and 1936, was an enthusiast of all things miniature. Her dollsã house even contains scale versions of nearly 600 real books in its library, including works by literary giants like A.A. Milne and Sir Arthur Conan Doyle. Commissioned from publishers around the U.K. and farther afield, many of these books are also held in the collections of the V&A, where they have been on long-term loan since 1916.



A recent video produced by the museum glimpses some of these tiny treasures with the expert guidance of National Art Library Assistant Librarian Amy McMullan and Catherine Yvard, Curator of National Art Library Collections. Examples include a red leather-bound Bible published in 1896 by Glasgow-based David Bryce & Son, in addition to a Quran, a collection of poems by Robert Burns, and more.
The National Art Library is housed within the V&A, and more than a million publications related to art, design, and performance comprise an archive that spans the 8th century to today.
Many more miniature books comprise the museumãs holdings, in addition to Queen Maryãs collection. Little almanacs in their embellished folios were published annually and included notable dates, such as sunrises and sunset times, holidays, and other practical information. Many of the titles sport gilt edges, marbled papers, and even metal cases that double as lockets so that they could be worn.
The collection includes diminutive dictionaries, a souvenir of The Great Exhibition of 1851 in Hyde Park, and childrenãs booksãincluding a number of tunnel books, or paper peep-shows. These accordion-style tomes look at first glance like any other publication, but they expand into long tunnels through which viewers can take in a layered, dimensional scene.

The oldest object in the V&Aãs collection is an early 1700s silver-bound miniature prayerbook in French thatãs embellished with the semi-precious stone lapis lazuli. And the tiniest is fittingly called The Smallest Book in the World, published in 2002 and measuring 2.4 by 2.9 millimeters. It was designed by a German typographer and is so tiny that itãs accompanied by a small pamphlet showing what you would see if you could page through the book. As McMullen explains, the physical size of the book begs an interesting question: ãIs it really a book if you canãt read it?ã
Visitors are welcome to peruse the online catalogue and interact with items in the collection in person in the V&A East Storehouseãs reading rooms. Explore more on the museumãs YouTube channel.

schema.headline: You'll Need a Magnifying Glass to Read Some of the World's Smallest Books at the V&A

title: Pete Hegseth Goes to War With  Press Photographers
description:
News
                The Pentagon reportedly banned photography at briefings because the Secretary of War complained about ãunfl
body:
News
                The Pentagon reportedly banned photography at briefings because the Secretary of War complained about ãunflatteringã photos. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on March 7, 2026 (photo by Roberto Schmidt/Getty Images)In these unbelievably trying times, as the United States stands embroiled in a string of scary and heartbreaking international conflicts, we as a people expect one thing above all else from our Defense Secretary: image control. Luckily, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, alleged war criminal and highest-ranking military official (under the President), has his priorities in order, and has banned press photographers from Pentagon briefings on the US-Israeli military conflict with Iran, ongoing since the end of February.ô According to the Washington Post, based on anonymous sources, the move to ban press photographers is a response to photographs that circulated following the March 2 briefing ã Hegsethãs first press address since June of last year ã which the Defense Secretary (sorry ::checks notes:: Secretary of War) found ãunflattering.ãIt is unclear whether one specific photo has led to this abrupt cessation of the First Amendment, or simply the totality of photos has somehow ruffled Hegseth. This is particularly shocking, given that, as a former Fox News co-host, literally the only qualification this man has to meet the varied and important responsibilities of his high office is the ability to have his picture taken."In order to use space in the Pentagon Briefing Room effectively, we are allowing one representative per news outlet if uncredentialed, excluding pool,ã Pentagon press secretary Kingsley Wilson said in a statement released on March 11. ãPhotographs from the briefings are immediately released online for the public and press to use.ãô Listen, yes, we are embroiled in a high-stakes military conflict with resounding implications for the future stability of the Middle East (at minimum), which incidentally runs exactly counter to the campaign promises that got our current commander-in-chief elected. But if it is so dang important for the press to report on it, why did they photograph Hegsethãs bad side? Do you truly expect the God of War ã sorry, Secretary of Defense ã to passively abide this act of aggression? As a high representative of a nation, it is Hegsethãs duty to defend our rights, except for the first one, I guess. The one about whether we get to keep our guns is really the important one, as we all know.ô And honestly, I donãt totally blame Hegseth on this. If I were accused of committing war crimes like blowing up the Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school in Iran, I wouldnãt want members of the Hague to have a current photo of me, either. 
                  
                      
                        News

schema.headline: Pete Hegseth Goes to War With  Press Photographers
